title: A funeral that taught me who someone had really been
summary: Reflect on a funeral or memorial gathering where what other mourners said revealed parts of the person you had never known, and how that changed how you remember them.
topic: What a funeral revealed about someone you thought you knew well
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) reflecting on a funeral or memorial where other people's accounts of the deceased revealed parts of them you had never known, and how that changed your memory of them. Use the past perfect, present perfect for accumulated reflection, a modal of speculation, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: At my uncle's funeral in Cairo last spring, a man in his eighties stood up and said something about my uncle that I am almost certain my own family had never heard…
If I had known the version of him that those friends knew, I would have asked him different questions while he was alive, but I had never imagined…
Although we like to think we know our family well, what a funeral often reveals is that we have only ever seen one of several rooms in a house with many doors…

hintVocab.meaningVi: đến viếng, bày tỏ kính trọng
nhận ra điều gì đó muộn màng
một khía cạnh không nói ra
coi ai là điều hiển nhiên

hintVocab.phrase: the person you thought you knew
hintVocab.meaningVi: người mà bạn tưởng mình biết

hintVocab.phrase: a different chapter of a life
hintVocab.meaningVi: một chương khác của cuộc đời

sampleText:
At my uncle Hisham's funeral in Cairo last spring, a man in his eighties whom I had never seen before stood up at the back of the room and said something about my uncle that I am almost certain my own family had never heard. He had been a colleague of my uncle's in the 1970s, in a small civil engineering office on Talaat Harb Street, and he wanted to tell us that for nearly two years my uncle had quietly paid part of the rent on the apartment where this man's mother had lived alone. He had done it through a third person and had refused to be thanked. The man had only learned the full story decades later, when his mother died. He had come to the funeral not to say goodbye to a friend — they had drifted apart in the 1980s — but to say something out loud that he had carried for thirty years.

I sat there and tried to fit this story into the version of my uncle I had grown up with. The uncle I knew was a quiet, sometimes impatient man who had not been particularly affectionate and who almost never talked about his work. He had been generous in obvious ways — paying for school books, arriving at family events with too much food — but I had not known him to do anything dramatic for a stranger. The eulogies that morning brought out three more stories of the same kind, told by people who had no reason to invent them and no relationship to one another. He must have been arranging quiet help for almost forty years. Although I had spent my entire life thinking of him as a person without secrets, I had been wrong in a very specific way: he had simply kept his most defining acts out of view of his family, which is a kind of secret of its own.

What I have come to understand, in the months since the funeral, is that we usually see a small selection of the people closest to us — the version that appears at our birthdays, at family lunches, on the phone on a Sunday evening. A funeral can be the first time several other versions are described in the same room. However, this is not only a story about late discovery. It is also a question that has stayed with me: how many of the people I see every week have a side of themselves that I will never see unless something dramatic happens, and would they want me to know? I have not landed on an answer. What has changed is the way I ask the older relatives I still have alive about parts of their lives I had never thought to wonder about.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect continuous for a long-running past activity
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'He must have been arranging quiet help for almost forty years' kết hợp 'must have been + -ing' để diễn đạt suy đoán mạnh về một hoạt động đã kéo dài trong quá khứ. So với 'must have arranged' (hành động xong), dạng tiếp diễn nhấn mạnh thời gian kéo dài.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'He must have been arranging quiet help for almost forty years' uses 'must have been + -ing' for confident deduction about a long-running past activity. Compared with 'must have arranged' (completed action), the continuous form emphasises duration.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect to set up the layers of a story
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng nhiều câu quá khứ hoàn thành ('had been', 'had paid', 'had refused', 'had carried', 'had grown up with') để dựng các sự kiện đã có sẵn trước đám tang. Đây là cách dùng cốt lõi của quá khứ hoàn thành trong văn tự sự — đánh dấu các sự kiện cũ hơn so với khoảnh khắc chính trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses many past perfect clauses ('had been', 'had paid', 'had refused', 'had carried', 'had grown up with') to set up events from before the funeral. This is the core use of past perfect in narrative writing — to mark events earlier than the main past moment.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tại đám tang chú Hisham của tôi ở Cairo mùa xuân năm ngoái, một người đàn ông ngoài tám mươi mà tôi chưa từng gặp trước đây đứng dậy ở phía sau phòng và nói điều gì đó về chú tôi mà tôi gần như chắc chắn chính gia đình tôi cũng chưa bao giờ nghe. Ông ấy đã từng là đồng nghiệp của chú tôi trong những năm 1970, ở một văn phòng kỹ thuật xây dựng nhỏ trên đường Talaat Harb, và ông muốn nói với chúng tôi rằng gần hai năm chú tôi đã âm thầm trả một phần tiền thuê căn hộ nơi mẹ ông sống một mình. Ông ấy đã làm điều đó qua một người thứ ba và đã từ chối được cảm ơn. Người đàn ông chỉ biết toàn bộ câu chuyện hàng thập kỷ sau, khi mẹ ông qua đời. Ông đến đám tang không phải để nói lời tạm biệt với một người bạn — họ đã xa cách trong những năm 1980 — mà để nói thành lời điều gì đó ông đã mang trong lòng ba mươi năm.

Tôi ngồi đó và cố lắp câu chuyện này vào phiên bản về chú tôi mà tôi đã lớn lên cùng. Người chú tôi biết là một người đàn ông ít nói, đôi khi thiếu kiên nhẫn, không đặc biệt trìu mến và gần như không bao giờ nói về công việc của mình. Ông đã hào phóng theo những cách rõ ràng — trả tiền cho sách giáo khoa, đến các sự kiện gia đình với quá nhiều đồ ăn — nhưng tôi không biết ông từng làm điều gì kịch tính cho một người lạ. Các bài điếu văn sáng hôm đó còn đưa ra ba câu chuyện cùng loại, được kể bởi những người không có lý do gì để bịa và không có quan hệ gì với nhau. Ông ấy hẳn đã sắp xếp sự giúp đỡ thầm lặng gần bốn mươi năm. Mặc dù tôi đã dành cả đời nghĩ về ông như một người không có bí mật, tôi đã sai theo một cách rất cụ thể: ông đơn giản đã giữ những hành động xác định ông nhất khỏi tầm nhìn của gia đình, đó tự nó là một loại bí mật.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu, trong những tháng kể từ đám tang, là chúng ta thường chỉ thấy một lựa chọn nhỏ của những người gần ta nhất — phiên bản xuất hiện trong sinh nhật của ta, trong các bữa trưa gia đình, trên điện thoại vào tối Chủ nhật. Một đám tang có thể là lần đầu tiên nhiều phiên bản khác được mô tả trong cùng một phòng. Tuy nhiên, đây không chỉ là câu chuyện về khám phá muộn màng. Đó cũng là một câu hỏi đã ở lại với tôi: có bao nhiêu người tôi gặp mỗi tuần có một khía cạnh của chính họ mà tôi sẽ không bao giờ thấy trừ khi điều gì đó kịch tính xảy ra, và liệu họ có muốn tôi biết? Tôi chưa đi đến một câu trả lời. Điều đã thay đổi là cách tôi hỏi những người thân lớn tuổi tôi vẫn còn sống về những phần của cuộc đời họ mà tôi chưa bao giờ nghĩ đến để tự hỏi.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'must have been + -ing' correctly?
options: He must have been arranging quiet help for almost forty years.
He must has been arranging quiet help for almost forty years.
He must been arranging quiet help for almost forty years.
He must have arranging quiet help for almost forty years.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Must have been + -ing' is the structure for confident deduction about an extended past activity.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: My uncle had quietly paid part of the rent on the apartment.
My uncle has quietly paid part of the rent on the apartment.
My uncle had quietly pay part of the rent on the apartment.
My uncle had been quietly pay part of the rent on the apartment.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; past participle of 'pay' is 'paid'.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; 'paid' is the past participle of 'pay'.

options: Although I had spent my entire life thinking of him as a person without secrets, I had been wrong in a very specific way.
Although I had spent my entire life thinking of him as a person without secrets, but I had been wrong in a very specific way.
Although, I had spent my entire life thinking of him as a person without secrets I had been wrong in a very specific way.
I had spent my entire life thinking of him as a person without secrets, although however I had been wrong in a very specific way.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whom' correctly in a non-defining relative clause?
options: A man in his eighties whom I had never seen before stood up at the back of the room.
A man in his eighties which I had never seen before stood up at the back of the room.
A man in his eighties whom I had never seen him before stood up at the back of the room.
A man in his eighties who I had never seen before him stood up at the back of the room.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whom' is the formal object form for people in relative clauses; no repeated object pronoun.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whom' for object position; do not repeat the object with a pronoun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the past perfect passive correctly?
options: He had refused to be thanked.
He had refused to thanked.
He had refused to being thanked.
He had refused to was thanked.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To be + past participle' is the passive infinitive after 'refuse to'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
options: What I have come to understand, in the months since the funeral, is that we usually see a small selection.
What I came to understand, in the months since the funeral, is that we usually see a small selection.
What I have came to understand, in the months since the funeral, is that we usually see a small selection.
What I have come understanding, in the months since the funeral, is that we usually see a small selection.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come to + base verb'; past participle of 'come' is 'come'.

options: I will never see this side of them unless something dramatic happens.
I will never see this side of them unless something dramatic will happen.
I will never see this side of them unless something dramatic happen.
I will never see this side of them if not something dramatic happens.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Unless' takes the present simple even for future reference; third-person singular ('happens'); 'if not' is informal and unusual here.
mcQuestions.hint: Time/condition clauses use present simple even for future; 'unless' = 'if not'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'tell + object + that-clause' correctly?
options: He wanted to tell us that for nearly two years my uncle had quietly paid part of the rent.
He wanted to tell to us that for nearly two years my uncle had quietly paid part of the rent.
He wanted to say us that for nearly two years my uncle had quietly paid part of the rent.
He wanted to tell us about that for nearly two years my uncle had quietly paid part of the rent.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tell + object + that-clause'; 'tell' takes a direct object without 'to'; 'say' does not take an indirect object directly.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tell us that…' (not 'tell to us' or 'say us').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'have no reason to + base verb' correctly?
options: Told by people who had no reason to invent them.
Told by people who had no reason of invent them.
Told by people who had no reason for invent them.
Told by people who had not reason to invent them.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have no reason to + base verb' is the standard structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'a kind of … of its own' correctly?
options: He had kept his most defining acts out of view of his family, which is a kind of secret of its own.
He had kept his most defining acts out of view of his family, which is a kind of secret of his own.
He had kept his most defining acts out of view of his family, which is a kind of secret of itself.
He had kept his most defining acts out of view of his family, which is kind a of secret of its own.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A kind of X of its own' means 'an X with its own distinctive character'. 'Of its own' (not 'of his own' here, because we are talking about the act, not the person).
mcQuestions.hint: 'Of its own' refers to an impersonal thing; 'of his own' would refer to him as a person.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'I have not landed on' correctly?
options: I have not landed on an answer.
I have not land on an answer.
I has not landed on an answer.
I have not landing on an answer.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = 'have + past participle'; past participle of 'land' is 'landed'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If funerals reveal sides of people that family members never saw, what would change about the way you treat the people close to you if you assumed they had at least one such hidden side right now?
